Approach 1: Rigetti Computing Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in today's terms.

For Rigetti Computing, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at a loss of about US$60.5 million. Analysts and model projections then extend this forward, with Simply Wall St using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The ten year path includes several years of negative cash flow before reaching an estimated Free Cash Flow of about US$405.7 million in 2030, with further extrapolated estimates out to 2035.

Pulling those cash flows back to today results in an estimated intrinsic value of US$45.35 per share, compared with the recent share price of US$24.96. On this model, Rigetti Computing screens as materially undervalued, with an implied discount of about 45.0%.

Approach 2: Rigetti Computing Price vs Book

For companies that are not yet profitable, traditional earnings based metrics like the P/E ratio are less useful. Investors often look at asset based measures such as the Price to Book, or P/B, ratio instead. This ratio compares what the market is paying for each dollar of net assets on the balance sheet.

A higher P/B ratio can reflect expectations of stronger growth or higher returns on those assets. However, it can also point to higher risk if future execution is uncertain. A lower P/B ratio can indicate more modest growth expectations or concerns around the quality of the asset base.

Rigetti Computing currently trades on a P/B of 22.15x. This is above both the Semiconductor industry average of 4.63x and the peer group average of 7.20x. This is the P/B level that would be consistent with the company’s earnings profile, growth outlook, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics.

Because the Fair Ratio blends these company specific drivers with industry context, it can be more informative than a simple comparison to peers or the broad industry. In Rigetti Computing's case, the current 22.15x P/B sits above this Fair Ratio, which points to the shares screening as overvalued on this measure.

data.title: Cadence Bank (CADE) Margin Slip To 28.6% Tests Bullish Growth Narrative
data.description:
Cadence Bank (CADE) has put up a clean set of FY 2025 numbers, with fourth quarter revenue at US$500.4 million and EPS at US$0.76, while trailing twelve month revenue came in at US$1.9 billion and EPS at US$2.83. Over recent periods the bank has seen revenue move from US$435.7 million in Q4 2024 to US$500.4 million in Q4 2025, with quarterly EPS stepping from US$0.70 to US$0.76. This sets the stage for investors to focus on how consistently the bank converts that income into margins and long term earnings power.

See our full analysis for Cadence Bank.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up against the prevailing narratives around Cadence Bank's growth, risks, and profitability quality.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Loan Book Growth With Stable Credit Quality

  • Total loans in the quarterly data move from US$33,741.8 million in Q4 2024 to US$36,801.8 million in Q3 2025, while non performing loans in that same snapshot range from US$264.7 million down to US$249.8 million. This shows the loan book getting larger alongside a slightly smaller pool of problem loans in the figures given.
  • Bulls often like the idea of a growing regional bank, and here the multi year earnings growth of 13.3% a year and forecast earnings growth of about 14% a year sit next to this larger loan base. The non performing loans numbers remind you to ask how much of that growth is coming from credit that still looks solid.
    • On one hand, the earnings growth history and the 2.52% dividend line up with the view that the franchise is adding business without obvious stress in the credit metrics in these snapshots.
    • On the other hand, the modest year over year net margin slip from 29.9% to 28.6% keeps a bullish view grounded, because it shows profitability not moving in lockstep with the loan and earnings growth story.

Margins Slip To 28.6% Net Profit

  • Net profit margin over the last year is 28.6%, compared with 29.9% the prior year, and within the quarterly data you see net income excluding extra items move around a relatively tight band from US$130.3 million in Q4 2024 to US$146.7 million in Q4 2025 as total revenue steps up to US$500.4 million.
  • Bears often worry that higher costs or tougher competition on loans and deposits could eat into profitability, and the 1.3 percentage point margin move together with net interest margin figures around 3.3% to 3.46% gives them some material to point to. The earnings growth track record adds an extra layer to that story.
    • Critics highlight that a margin line of 28.6% versus 29.9% suggests less profit being kept from each revenue dollar at the same time the bank is earning US$532.6 million in trailing twelve month net income excluding extra items.
    • What stands out is that this margin softness is happening alongside that same 13.3% multi year earnings growth rate, which challenges a simple bearish claim that profitability is weakening across the board.

P/E Premium Versus Peers With DCF Gap

  • Cadence trades on a P/E of 15.3x against peer and US Banks industry averages of 12.4x and 12.1x. The provided DCF fair value of US$71.85 sits well above the current share price of US$43.70, so the stock is shown as trading around 39% below that DCF fair value while still carrying a premium P/E multiple and a 2.52% dividend yield.
  • Bulls argue that multi year earnings growth of 13.3% a year plus forecast earnings growth near 14% and the indicated gap to DCF fair value justify paying a richer multiple. The numbers also give you a way to test how much of that optimism is already reflected in the current price.
    • Supporters can point to trailing twelve month revenue of about US$1.9b and net income excluding extra items of US$532.6 million as evidence that the bank is already earning well against that US$43.70 share price.
    • At the same time, anyone cautious can see that this is not a low multiple name on a P/E basis relative to peers, which keeps the valuation debate very much tied to whether those earnings growth rates and cash flow assumptions behind the DCF fair value hold up over time.
To see how these valuation signals, growth rates, and margin trends fit into a fuller story for Cadence Bank, you might want to read the balanced narrative that pulls these pieces together before you decide what they mean for your own approach. 📊 Read the full Cadence Bank Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Benefit Systems S.A. (WSE:BFT) is a favorite amongst institutional investors who own 63%
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Significantly high institutional ownership implies Benefit Systems' stock price is sensitive to their trading actions
  • The top 7 shareholders own 54% of the company
  • Analyst forecasts along with ownership data serve to give a strong idea about prospects for a business

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

A look at the shareholders of Benefit Systems S.A. (WSE:BFT) can tell us which group is most powerful. The group holding the most number of shares in the company, around 63% to be precise, is institutions. In other words, the group stands to gain the most (or lose the most) from their investment into the company.

Because institutional owners have a huge pool of resources and liquidity, their investing decisions tend to carry a great deal of weight, especially with individual investors. Therefore, a good portion of institutional money invested in the company is usually a huge vote of confidence on its future.

Let's take a closer look to see what the different types of shareholders can tell us about Benefit Systems.

Check out our latest analysis for Benefit Systems

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Benefit Systems?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

As you can see, institutional investors have a fair amount of stake in Benefit Systems. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. If multiple institutions change their view on a stock at the same time, you could see the share price drop fast. It's therefore worth looking at Benefit Systems' earnings history below. Of course, the future is what really matters.

Since institutional investors own more than half the issued stock, the board will likely have to pay attention to their preferences. Hedge funds don't have many shares in Benefit Systems. Looking at our data, we can see that the largest shareholder is Benefit Invest Ltd with 13% of shares outstanding. Meanwhile, the second and third largest shareholders, hold 9.6% and 8.4%, of the shares outstanding, respectively.

We did some more digging and found that 7 of the top shareholders account for roughly 54% of the register, implying that along with larger shareholders, there are a few smaller shareholders, thereby balancing out each others interests somewhat.

While studying institutional ownership for a company can add value to your research, it is also a good practice to research analyst recommendations to get a deeper understand of a stock's expected performance. Quite a few analysts cover the stock, so you could look into forecast growth quite easily.

Insider Ownership Of Benefit Systems

The definition of company insiders can be subjective and does vary between jurisdictions. Our data reflects individual insiders, capturing board members at the very least. The company management answer to the board and the latter should represent the interests of shareholders. Notably, sometimes top-level managers are on the board themselves.

Most consider insider ownership a positive because it can indicate the board is well aligned with other shareholders. However, on some occasions too much power is concentrated within this group.

We can report that insiders do own shares in Benefit Systems S.A.. This is a big company, so it is good to see this level of alignment. Insiders own zł832m worth of shares (at current prices). It is good to see this level of investment by insiders. You can check here to see if those insiders have been buying recently.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 12% stake in Benefit Systems. While this group can't necessarily call the shots, it can certainly have a real influence on how the company is run.

Private Company Ownership

Our data indicates that Private Companies hold 13%, of the company's shares. Private companies may be related parties. Sometimes insiders have an interest in a public company through a holding in a private company, rather than in their own capacity as an individual. While it's hard to draw any broad stroke conclusions, it is worth noting as an area for further research.

Next Steps:

I find it very interesting to look at who exactly owns a company. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. Take risks for example - Benefit Systems has 1 warning sign we think you should be aware of.

If you are like me, you may want to think about whether this company will grow or shrink. Luckily, you can check this free report showing analyst forecasts for its future.

data.title: OptiCept Technologies (STO:OPTI) Is Carrying A Fair Bit Of Debt
data.description:
Howard Marks put it nicely when he said that, rather than worrying about share price volatility, 'The possibility of permanent loss is the risk I worry about... and every practical investor I know worries about.' When we think about how risky a company is, we always like to look at its use of debt, since debt overload can lead to ruin. Importantly, OptiCept Technologies AB (publ) (STO:OPTI) does carry debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

When Is Debt A Problem?

Debt is a tool to help businesses grow, but if a business is incapable of paying off its lenders, then it exists at their mercy. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, plenty of companies use debt to fund growth, without any negative consequences. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

What Is OptiCept Technologies's Net Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 OptiCept Technologies had debt of kr21.7m, up from kr20.0m in one year. On the flip side, it has kr3.62m in cash leading to net debt of about kr18.1m.

A Look At OptiCept Technologies' Liabilities

Zooming in on the latest balance sheet data, we can see that OptiCept Technologies had liabilities of kr55.4m due within 12 months and liabilities of kr5.07m due beyond that. Offsetting this, it had kr3.62m in cash and kr34.3m in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by kr22.6m.

Since publicly traded OptiCept Technologies shares are worth a total of kr143.0m, it seems unlikely that this level of liabilities would be a major threat. However, we do think it is worth keeping an eye on its balance sheet strength, as it may change over time. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if OptiCept Technologies can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you want to see what the professionals think, you might find this free report on analyst profit forecasts to be interesting.

Check out our latest analysis for OptiCept Technologies

In the last year OptiCept Technologies wasn't profitable at an EBIT level, but managed to grow its revenue by 30%, to kr43m. With any luck the company will be able to grow its way to profitability.

Caveat Emptor

Even though OptiCept Technologies managed to grow its top line quite deftly, the cold hard truth is that it is losing money on the EBIT line. Its EBIT loss was a whopping kr54m. Considering that alongside the liabilities mentioned above does not give us much confidence that company should be using so much debt. Quite frankly we think the balance sheet is far from match-fit, although it could be improved with time. Another cause for caution is that is bled kr58m in negative free cash flow over the last twelve months. So suffice it to say we consider the stock very risky. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. data.title: Estimating The Fair Value Of Scout24 SE (ETR:G24)
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • The projected fair value for Scout24 is €99.30 based on 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity
  • Current share price of €85.05 suggests Scout24 is potentially trading close to its fair value
  • Analyst price target for G24 is €121, which is 22% above our fair value estimate

Today we will run through one way of estimating the intrinsic value of Scout24 SE (ETR:G24) by estimating the company's future cash flows and discounting them to their present value. The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is the tool we will apply to do this. Don't get put off by the jargon, the math behind it is actually quite straightforward.

Remember though, that there are many ways to estimate a company's value, and a DCF is just one method. If you still have some burning questions about this type of valuation, take a look at the Simply Wall St analysis model.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Is Scout24 Fairly Valued?

We use what is known as a 2-stage model, which simply means we have two different periods of growth rates for the company's cash flows. Generally the first stage is higher growth, and the second stage is a lower growth phase. To start off with, we need to estimate the next ten years of cash flows. Where possible we use analyst estimates, but when these aren't available we extrapolate the previous free cash flow (FCF) from the last estimate or reported value. We assume companies with shrinking free cash flow will slow their rate of shrinkage, and that companies with growing free cash flow will see their growth rate slow, over this period. We do this to reflect that growth tends to slow more in the early years than it does in later years.

A DCF is all about the idea that a dollar in the future is less valuable than a dollar today, so we discount the value of these future cash flows to their estimated value in today's dollars:

10-year free cash flow (FCF) forecast

2026202720282029203020312032203320342035
Levered FCF (€, Millions) €284.7m€326.9m€335.0m€341.8m€348.3m€354.5m€360.6m€366.7m€372.7m€378.7m
Growth Rate Estimate SourceAnalyst x7Analyst x8Analyst x1Est @ 2.03%Est @ 1.89%Est @ 1.79%Est @ 1.72%Est @ 1.67%Est @ 1.64%Est @ 1.62%
Present Value (€, Millions) Discounted @ 6.2% €268€290€280€269€258€247€237€227€217€208

("Est" = FCF growth rate estimated by Simply Wall St)
Present Value of 10-year Cash Flow (PVCF) = €2.5b

We now need to calculate the Terminal Value, which accounts for all the future cash flows after this ten year period. The Gordon Growth formula is used to calculate Terminal Value at a future annual growth rate equal to the 5-year average of the 10-year government bond yield of 1.6%. We discount the terminal cash flows to today's value at a cost of equity of 6.2%.

Terminal Value (TV)= FCF2035 × (1 + g) ÷ (r – g) = €379m× (1 + 1.6%) ÷ (6.2%– 1.6%) = €8.3b

Present Value of Terminal Value (PVTV)= TV / (1 + r)10= €8.3b÷ ( 1 + 6.2%)10= €4.6b

The total value is the sum of cash flows for the next ten years plus the discounted terminal value, which results in the Total Equity Value, which in this case is €7.1b. In the final step we divide the equity value by the number of shares outstanding. Compared to the current share price of €85.1, the company appears about fair value at a 14% discount to where the stock price trades currently. Valuations are imprecise instruments though, rather like a telescope - move a few degrees and end up in a different galaxy. Do keep this in mind.

Important Assumptions

We would point out that the most important inputs to a discounted cash flow are the discount rate and of course the actual cash flows. If you don't agree with these result, have a go at the calculation yourself and play with the assumptions. The DCF also does not consider the possible cyclicality of an industry, or a company's future capital requirements, so it does not give a full picture of a company's potential performance. Given that we are looking at Scout24 as potential shareholders, the cost of equity is used as the discount rate, rather than the cost of capital (or weighted average cost of capital, WACC) which accounts for debt. In this calculation we've used 6.2%, which is based on a levered beta of 1.097. Beta is a measure of a stock's volatility, compared to the market as a whole. We get our beta from the industry average beta of globally comparable companies, with an imposed limit between 0.8 and 2.0, which is a reasonable range for a stable business.

View our latest analysis for Scout24

SWOT Analysis for Scout24

Strength
  • Earnings growth over the past year exceeded the industry.
  • Debt is not viewed as a risk.
  • Dividends are covered by earnings and cash flows.
    Dividend information for G24.
Weakness
  • Dividend is low compared to the top 25% of dividend payers in the Interactive Media and Services market.
Opportunity
  • Annual revenue is forecast to grow faster than the German market.
  • Current share price is below our estimate of fair value.
Threat
  • Annual earnings are forecast to grow slower than the German market.
    What else are analysts forecasting for G24?

Moving On:

Although the valuation of a company is important, it ideally won't be the sole piece of analysis you scrutinize for a company. The DCF model is not a perfect stock valuation tool. Rather it should be seen as a guide to "what assumptions need to be true for this stock to be under/overvalued?" If a company grows at a different rate, or if its cost of equity or risk free rate changes sharply, the output can look very different. While markets are a powerful pricing mechanism, share prices reflect investor sentiment, not just underlying business performance. By comparing earnings per share (EPS) and share price changes over time, we can get a feel for how investor attitudes to a company have morphed over time.

During five years of share price growth, Apollo Tyres achieved compound earnings per share (EPS) growth of 18% per year. This EPS growth is reasonably close to the 20% average annual increase in the share price. That suggests that the market sentiment around the company hasn't changed much over that time. What About Dividends?

It is important to consider the total shareholder return, as well as the share price return, for any given stock. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. Arguably, the TSR gives a more comprehensive picture of the return generated by a stock. As it happens, Apollo Tyres' TSR for the last 5 years was 166%, which exceeds the share price return mentioned earlier. And there's no prize for guessing that the dividend payments largely explain the divergence!

Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for LIMES Schlosskliniken:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.19 = €7.0m ÷ (€44m - €6.4m) (Based on the trailing twelve months to June 2025).

The Trend Of ROCE

data.title: Antero Resources Weighs Growth Potential Against Higher Debt In HG Energy Deal
data.description:
• Antero Resources (NYSE:AR) has agreed to acquire HG Energy II in a cash deal valued at about $2.8b.
  • The company is funding the transaction with a new $750m senior notes offering and additional debt.
  • The acquisition marks a major expansion for Antero Resources and a significant change to its capital structure.

Antero Resources, trading around $34.38, is making a sizable move with the HG Energy II purchase, which stands out against a 3 year return of 17.8% and a very large 5 year gain. The stock is up 8.2% over the past week, while the 1 year return shows a 15.2% decline, so this deal is landing after a mixed stretch for shareholders.

For investors, the key questions now are how the added $2.8b of assets and the new debt load interact over time. The transaction puts more focus on Antero's ability to manage its balance sheet and extract value from the acquired portfolio, which could influence how the market views NYSE:AR in the coming quarters.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Antero Resources by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Antero Resources.

Why Antero Resources could be great value

The HG Energy II deal is a sizeable bet on growth, and the funding mix tells you a lot about how Antero Resources is choosing to structure that bet. The company has locked in US$750m of 5.400% senior subordinated unsecured notes due 2036, priced just under par at 99.869%, and paired this with a US$1.5b three year term loan. Together, these represent a clear step up in leverage to secure US$2.8b of assets.

Advertisement

Antero Resources Narrative, Debt Fueled Expansion in Focus

Recent analyst commentary, including multiple Hold or neutral style ratings and a modestly reduced price target from one major firm, suggests investors are still weighing how this acquisition and added debt sit within the broader Antero story, which already includes mixed share price performance over the past year but strong longer term returns.

Risks and Rewards of the HG Energy II Deal

  • ⚠️ Higher interest obligations from the 5.400% notes and the US$1.5b term loan increase Antero Resources' fixed financial commitments.
  • ⚠️ A larger debt load can reduce flexibility if commodity prices or operating conditions turn less favorable.
  • 🎁 The acquisition adds US$2.8b of assets that could support higher production and cash flow over time if integrated efficiently.
  • 🎁 The long dated 2036 maturity on the notes helps lock in funding terms for a decade, which can provide planning visibility.

What Investors May Want To Watch Next

From here, it will be important to watch how quickly Antero Resources pays down the new borrowings, how the acquired assets contribute to cash generation, and how analyst sentiment evolves. You can stay plugged into the debate by reading fresh community views in this ongoing narrative.

data.title: Applied Digital Expands AI Factory Footprint As Co Founder Becomes President
data.description:
• Applied Digital (NasdaqGS:APLD) has started construction on its new Delta Forge 1 AI Factory campus, planned to begin operations in mid-2027.
  • The company is expanding its high-density AI data center footprint to serve hyperscale customers with large scale, purpose built capacity.
  • Applied Digital has appointed co founder Jason Zhang as President, adding investment and technology experience to its leadership team.

For you as an investor, the story around Applied Digital is increasingly about AI infrastructure rather than just traditional data center capacity. The Delta Forge 1 campus sits in the broader trend of rising demand for power dense facilities that can support large AI workloads for hyperscale clients.

Looking ahead, the combination of a sizable new build and a leadership shift gives you more concrete milestones to track, from construction progress through to the planned 2027 operations start. How effectively NasdaqGS:APLD converts construction and leadership changes into contracted capacity and execution will be a key focus for the market.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Applied Digital by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Applied Digital.

How Applied Digital stacks up against its biggest competitors

The Delta Forge 1 project signals that Applied Digital is leaning further into very large scale AI infrastructure, with up to 300 MW of critical IT load planned and room to expand. For you, the key question is how quickly the company can turn that planned 430 MW of utility power into contracted, revenue producing capacity with investment grade hyperscale customers.

Advertisement

Applied Digital narrative, from crypto roots to AI infrastructure builder

This move extends the existing narrative of Applied Digital shifting from bitcoin mining toward high performance AI data centers, supported by long term leases like the 200 MW agreement at Polaris Forge 2 and a reported US$16b in prospective lease revenue tied to 600 MW of capacity. Jason Zhang stepping in as President fits that story of continuity around securing and executing large campus deals, given his role in negotiating prior leases and his background as a technology investor.

Risks and rewards for investors to weigh

  • Delta Forge 1 adds meaningful potential capacity on top of the current North American footprint, which could appeal to hyperscalers seeking power dense AI sites.
  • The Macquarie preferred equity facility and institutional backing, including NVIDIA, give Applied Digital additional funding options for large projects.
  • The business remains capital intensive and debt heavy, so construction timelines, financing terms and utilization levels are important for you to watch.
  • The stock has attracted high short interest and has shown volatility, which can amplify market moves around any execution setbacks.

What to watch next

From here, you may want to track progress milestones at Delta Forge 1, how quickly any new hyperscale agreements are signed for that campus, and how Zhang’s expanded role influences deal making and execution discipline. If you want a broader sense of how other investors are interpreting these moves, you can check out what the community is saying about Applied Digital and similar AI infrastructure names.
